knobs.narration_genre: Out of the Abyss is an Underdark survival picaresque narrated in close third-person past, one POV per section, with the heading naming the character. The prose breathes with whoever is holding the section — Daz's dry Menzoberranzan audit ("at least we have that"), Grygum's patient orc-cleric reframing ("this is not the will of any god, this is the will of the people"), Thorin's giant-raised heart-on-sleeve bluffness ("ominous sounds fun"), Zalthir's exasperated monk economy ("I sense some racial animus"). The fifth voice in every scene is the Underdark itself — low ceilings, scarce water, no sky, Faerzress fields that warp the mind, and the running awareness that a tunnel can put an enemy in front of you that was behind you an hour ago. Tone shifts scene to scene: tactical problem-solving (which cantrip, which tunnel, who carries Stool), dark cultural-collision comedy (orc cleric of a dragon god negotiating with kuo-toan archpriests of a scavenger goddess), procedural D&D combat with spell names and tactical reasoning visible inside the character's reaction, and sudden cosmic-horror beats when a demon lord rises out of the Darklake and the prose has nowhere to hide. Dialogue is broken in with minimal tags; the ensemble of fellow escapees (Jimjar's "Wanna bet?", Buppido's "divine plan," Shuushar's enlightenment patter, Stool's helpfulness) functions as a comic chorus and should never be sanitized. The narrator never editorializes — characters speak the stakes themselves or the stakes go unspoken.
